data.title: GBTC: Great Hedge Against Inflation
data.description:
Introduction

The price of bitcoin is currently above 100k, more specifically at 104k. It is about 7k above its all-time high, achieved at 111,951 on May 28, 2025. In today's article, we will analyze in detail the Bitcoin asset and its possible path in the short term. With the aim of making a buy/sell or maintenance recommendation on the investment vehicle Grayscale Bitcoin Trust (NYSEARCA:GBTC).

The GBTC is an investment vehicle (ETP) owned by the investment firm Grayscale that offers a direct exposure to the Bitcoin asset, without the investor having to buy, store or guard it. Being a passive trust, it is not subject to the same financial regulations as a Bitcoin ETF when used. The vehicle always keeps a fixed amount of Bitcoin and issues shares that reflect that holding of them. They do not buy and sell Bitcoin on the market, unless the fund's Authorized Participants (PAs) create or redeem what are known as creation units (creation units).

For example, to create new shares (creation units) an Authorized Participant delivers to the trust a multiple of 25,000 shares in Bitcoins (in kind), i.e. approximately 25,000 × 0.00078771 ≈ 19.7 BTC per unit; in exchange, it receives new shares from the trust. To redeem these actions, the Authorized Participant performs the same process but in reverse. As the reader will understand, each new share issued increases the vehicle's reserves by approximately 0.00078771 Bitcoin.

The relationship could be summarized in the following equation:

Where are we?

Currently Bitcoin is in its third bullish cycle after those of the years 2015, 2018 and 2022. With increased institutional participation in daily Bitcoin purchases triggered by the approval of the different ETFs since January 10, 2024. With the entry of this smarter type of capital, this last cycle is lengthening and probably lasts until the end of this year, having a slighter correction, due to the maturation of this market.

Analysis - Macro & On - Chain

In this section, we will analyze Bitcoin from a macroeconomic and onchain point of view, to facilitate that buy/sell or maintenance recommendation.

First we start by looking at the DXY. The reason for this, is that as seen in the following chart, the level of the DXY clearly marks the end of the Bitcoin cycle. As can be seen in the image, in both 2018 and 2022 the DXY levels were around $88, coinciding with the local highs of the respective cycles in Bitcoin. With respect to the current moment we appreciate that the DXY is at about $100 and decreasing, probably going to look for the levels of 85 -90 that coincide with the maximum in the price of Bitcoin in this cycle.

Secondly, we present the Bitcoin vs MAG 7 Ratio metric. This metric compares how many times Bitcoin's cumulative yield exceeds or doesn't exceed the performance of the Magnificent 7. It is clear from the chart that the ratio peaks coincide with the local peaks for each asset cycle. Currently, the metric comes in a clear uptrend where Bitcoin is currently outperforming the MAG 7 by 5 to 1. From my point of view, as long as this indicator is below 10, the current bullish cycle will remain active and the price of Bitcoin will increase from current levels until it reaches that yield multiple.

After these two metrics, we will begin to analyze Bitcoin from the perspective of the activity of your chain. As the first metric in this section we present the Supply in Profit Trend metric. What we analyze in this metric is what the trend of the supply of both LTH and STH is currently in profits. As you can see in the chart, today more than 90% of Bitcoin's circulating supply (20,000,000) is in profits. This has historically been an early sign of future sales of the asset, so we could initially foresee an imminent price contraction. But there's one peculiarity at the moment, which is the third time in less than 6 months that the metric has reached those levels. For this reason, we rule out a short-term correction and predict a rise in the price in search of new highs, since corrections for this reason already existed at these price levels.

In the graph below, we can see the AVIV Momentum Trend Indicator metric. The active value to investor value (AVIV), relates on a daily basis the flow of currencies that change hands daily in the market, divided by the number of currencies that remain in the hands of investors. When the ratio is greater than 1, it means that the transacted flow exceeds the flow maintained by investors. As seen in the chart, when the ratio exceeds value 2, the asset enters a strong distribution phase. This metric does not distinguish between buy and sell within the transactional flow, so a ratio above 1 should not be taken as detrimental. At the moment, the AVIV ratio is at a value of 1.5, which I consider quite healthy to expose me to the asset today.

The next metric analyzed is the STH - Market Value Realized Value Momentum. This metric relates the market value and realized value of STHs (Short Term Holders), which are investors with coins less than 155 days old. The activity of these investors is important since they are the most influential on the price of Bitcoin in the short term. Currently the metric is at healthy levels, with good momentum (green bars at the base of the chart), which indicates a good time to buy.

Finally, we show our star metric that reflects momentum signals in relation to multiple onchain metrics. The blue bars on the chart mean good buying moments based on the momentum that the metric sets. On the other hand, the orange bars indicate otherwise; weak momentum. As you can see in the graph, we are currently at a good point according to the activity of the chain, which consequently gives us a positive signal of momentum, which I translate as a good entry point.

Conclusion

As a conclusion and to finish the article, if I recommend buying the GBTC vehicle that exposes you directly to Bitcoin. I believe that the current moment is a good entry point and that sooner rather than later the asset will seek new historical highs (ATH), which will benefit the vehicle analyzed. If you already bought and have it in your portfolio, my advice is to keep it for these reasons and because it acts fantastically as a hedge against inflation and the loss of purchasing power of traditional currencies.

data.title: FLOT: No Duration, But Still A Dollar Bet
data.description:
The iShares Floating Rate Bond ETF (BATS:FLOT) is a zero-duration fixed income ETF. This means that in effect there are limited credit risks with most of the ratings around A, and there is no duration risk, meaning no real sensitivity to changes in the yield curve. FLOT is the sort of ETF to typically pick up when you're not sure what else to do. However, we are in a quite rare instance of a rather important fall in the value of the dollar and it is related to trading factors concerning the USD reserve currency status. In other words, it is riskier than usual. The FLOT, while not a Treasury exposure, is exposed to US bonds (USD denominated). We do wonder about the impact of geopolitical developments, specifically the situation around Iran, and how that might impact the dollar. We are more inclined to believe that FLOT, which in effect is a yielding (productive) dollar bet, actually benefits from involvement of the US in the Iranian conflict should that come to pass, as it will demonstrate that the US is still concerning itself with the maritime order (Strait of Hormuz risks) and with the general geopolitical world order which supports the dollar. At the very least, even without the interaction between the USD and the geopolitical considerations, the reversal in oil will nominally mean more yield on USD debt as cuts will likely be pushed out more than hoped, which should support the USD. This reduces the risk to FLOT of being USD denominated.

FLOT Breakdown

FLOT is a little more intensively managed as it needs to keep the duration very low. The expense ratio is 0.15%, which is about in line for the more unusual horizons offered by iShares fixed income ETFs that we've seen. The effective duration is virtually zero, meaning that the yield of the ETF is basically variable, and reflects almost a spot interest rate. Moreover, the debt in FLOT is not just Treasury debt, it's of other institutions as well, mostly in banking and substantially in development banks.

Therefore, there is no issue of duration risk which comes from the relative value of a fixed coupon rising or declining depending on the prevailing rate environment after a bond's issuance. Given that there have been significant changes in the position of the yield curve, avoiding such risks can make sense for any capital allocator.

The significant changes in the yield curve in this instance do relate also to effects on the dollar. While USD denominated investors may not notice as much, USD has been performing rather badly, and it is related to the fact that the change in policy of the US, interpreted through the protectionism and economic nationalism of the US, particularly in the rhetoric of the tariffs and less so in the tariffs themselves, might signal conditions that would reduce the utility of the dollar as a reserve currency. The question around the US reserve currency status has been a trading factor and it has been affecting in particular the long maturity bonds of the US, as the US depends heavily on the USD's utility to be able to generate interest in their debt and to refinance.

The tariffs themselves, to the extent that they might be targeted at major flows from China which is a major US creditor, reduce the exchange of dollars to China that need to be invested somehow. Usually it would be in USD debt, but the risk is that might gradually change for alternative deposits in different currencies. But the other question is around the maritime order. The powerful US military has often acted as a guarantor of free movement of trade flows in the seas. With the US combining its trade protectionism with a foreign policy where they are stating they want to do less for allies in terms of hard power, the inclination towards the USD further reduces, as tacitly the use of the USD as a reserve as widely as it has been was a way to provide the financing base for the US in executing its role as a guarantor of freer maritime trade through the US military budget. The relationship is not particularly arcane and is defining of our times, which is to say that the USD's value is mostly from its reserve currency status and the immense value that the USD provides as an efficient medium of value, and little from the fundamentals of the US balance sheet and the typical dynamics one takes when valuing a currency.

Bottom Line

The latest development has been conflict in the Middle East. This poses risks that would mean avoiding duration is a smart idea. We just covered an ETF that was exposed to duration risk, and we predicted the position that the Fed has just taken to not cut rates yet. While the latest CPI readings were moderate, it was given a major helping hand by the lower oil price where the rule of thumb is a $10 decline in oil prices means a -0.4% or so deflationary vector on the CPI. That benefit has now evaporated as questions around Iran's direct oil supply, as well as risks to more major flows that depend on a clear Strait of Hormuz, have lifted the price of oil once again. While the demand environment remains tepid, these supply considerations have been important. Anything that pushes rate cuts out is bad for duration, and the conflict-ridden environment pressures long-term yields as it signals worse international cooperation which is benignly (productively) deflationary.

However, for FLOT, which doesn't concern itself with duration risks, the conflict in Iran provides an opportunity for the new US administration to demonstrate its actual-fact view of foreign military intervention vis-a-vis a national ally in Israel. With the starting position of the US that Iran should surrender, and Trump stating that his patience is wearing thin, and with Israel having established apparent air superiority for the moment, there is the possibility that the US gets involved even if in some limited way to provide its specialised assets against bunkers that support the Iranian nuclear programme. We believe that the interaction between the conflict and the key risk around the Strait of Hormuz means that involvement in this conflict could reinforce the US' commitment to maintaining some of the world order, in particular the maritime world order, which could support the dollar and revert assumptions the market is making about the new US administration. The dollar has climbed a little in the last couple of days back to levels on the 9th of June, possibly in part due to these factors.

However, there is also a general and less politically inclined reason to not be so bearish on the dollar at the moment and that is for the aforementioned reasons that the reversal in oil is going to push out rate cuts, which improves dollar yields and makes it US debt nominally attractive. We'd consider this a baseline benefit, though we think that a positive change in attitudes towards the USD due to their hard power commitments would do more for the dollar thesis. Otherwise, FLOT remains less attractive as the USD risk is still there even if there is no duration risk.

data.title: Intuitive Surgical: Poor Return Prospects Despite Near-Term Upside From Da Vinci 5
data.description:
Intuitive Surgical's (NASDAQ:ISRG) growth continues to accelerate driven by the rollout of the da Vinci 5 system, and increased adoption of the company's SP and Ion solutions. Procedure growth also remains robust, despite headwinds in bariatric surgery and a potential exhaustion of COVID related surgery backlogs. Growth investments are weighing on margins at the moment though. A situation that could be exacerbated by tariffs. This is only a temporary headwind, and Intuitive Surgical's margins should soon return to 30% plus levels.

I previously suggested that second half guidance would be critical to maintaining euphoric investor sentiment toward Intuitive Surgical's stock. I continue to think that Intuitive's valuation will not matter while investor risk appetite persists, and the company continues to outperform expectations. Despite solid growth, a long growth runway and a large competitive advantage, Intuitive Surgical's valuation will make it difficult for the company to generate strong returns longer term though.

Market Conditions

Procedure growth was strong in the quarter, even though there were fewer business days in the quarter. Procedures in the US increased 13% YoY, despite ongoing bariatric surgery headwinds (mid single digit decline). International procedures grew 24%, supported by solid growth in India, Korea and the UK. This is a positive given that strikes are negatively impacting Intuitive's business in South Korea.

Intuitive Surgical is facing headwinds in China though, which could be exacerbated by recent trade tensions. The Chinese government wants to localize production in a number of critical areas, including robotic surgery, which is making life difficult for foreign companies. While this is only impacting a small part of Intuitive's business at the moment, Chinese companies could become a serious threat in other price sensitive international markets.

Competition is also set to increase outside of China in coming years. In particular, Johnson and Johnson and Medtronic are investing heavily in soft tissue robotic systems. J&J completed the acquisition of Verb Surgical in 2020, a joint venture with Google's Verily subsidiary. This initiative eventually led to the OTTAVA robotic surgical system, which is now approaching commercialization. J&J recently completed the first cases with its OTTAVA robotic surgical system and now plans on submitting a De Novo authorization in the US. J&J also acquired Auris Health in 2019, a developer of robotic systems used in bronchoscopic diagnostic and therapeutic procedures. This enables the company to compete with both the da Vinci and Ion systems.

Medtronic offers the Hugo system, although this is not commercially available in the US yet. Hugo met safety and effectiveness endpoints in a recent US urologic trial though, and Medtronic has submitted the system for FDA approval. It has been suggested that Hugo lacks differentiation versus da Vinci, which could impact demand. This could also be a deliberate choice by Medtronic, designed to address the barrier created by user familiarity with Intuitive's system.

Competition probably won't matter in many markets, as Intuitive is already entrenched. It could make winning expansion markets more difficult, or at least ensure that these markets have less favorable economics.

Intuitive Surgical's systems were used in around 2.7 million procedures in 2024, which is something like 1% share of all surgeries globally (over 200 million surgeries each year). Current products and clearances enable Intuitive Surgical to target around 8 million procedures annually, although there are roughly 22 million soft tissue procedures that the company could eventually address. While this is a large opportunity, it isn't actually that large considering Intuitive's current valuation. There is likely to be a large additional growth opportunity in low income countries though. For example, surgery rates in countries with high health expenditures are over 30x higher than in countries with low health expenditures. Intuitive doesn't have the advantage of a large installed base and users that are familiar with its system in many of these countries, which will make capitalizing on this opportunity much more difficult.

Intuitive Surgical Business Updates

Intuitive's installed base exceeded 10,000 systems in Q1. The company placed 367 da Vinci systems in the quarter, including 147 da Vinci 5 systems (down from 174 systems in Q4) and 19 SP systems. The US was an area of strength in terms of system placements, although performance was mixed internationally. Trade-ins increased 130% YoY to 67, which is being driven by da Vinci 5 upgrades.

Intuitive placed 147 da Vinci 5 systems in the first quarter, bringing its installed base to 509 systems. Supply is still being limited until the company is confident it can properly support a broad rollout. Manufacturing capacity is ramping though, and Intuitive is planning a full launch around the middle of the year.

Intuitive is also targeting 510(k) clearance later in the year for features like real-time surgical video review, real-time 3D model review and visual force feedback gauges. Force feedback is designed to help reduce tissue injuries and shorten the learning curve of the system. Early data suggests that force feedback improves the proficiency of less experienced surgeons, leading to improved patient outcomes. Force feedback instruments are in limited supply though and will only be broadly available later in the year.

FDA clearance was recently expanded for the da Vinci Single Port system. It now includes transanal local excision/resection, adding to prior clearance for transabdominal, transoral, and urologic procedures. Intuitive Surgical is also enrolling additional patients in its IDE for nipple-sparing mastectomy.

Intuitive Surgical is focused on getting customers to use Ion for obtaining tissue samples from the lung (transthoracic needle aspiration biopsies), and believes that it is approaching 50% penetration of lung biopsies in the US. While Ion is currently being driven by lung cancer biopsies, its use will be expanded over time, supporting growth of the system longer term.

Tariffs could have a meaningful impact of Intuitive Surgical's business, both in terms of costs and sales. Intuitive manufactured 98% of its robotic systems in the US in 2024. The company also has manufacturing operations in Europe (70% of endoscopes) and Mexico (80% of instruments and accessories).

While there is significant uncertainty surrounding what tariffs will be implemented and when, Intuitive has given guidance that its cost of sales will rise by 1.7% of revenue in 2025. The negative impact will increase through the year though, meaning the rate exiting the year could be meaningfully higher.

Around half of the impact is related to US-China trade. Tariffs on imports into China could have a significant impact on Intuitive Surgical's ability to successfully compete. Intuitive also imports components from China into the US, which could be subject to high tariffs.

Most of Intuitive Surgical's imports from Mexico are certified under the requirements of USMCA. A small minority of imports are expected to incur a 25% tariff though. Other imports into the US are likely to be subject to lower tariffs.

Potential mitigation actions include optimizing production costs and rebalancing production across the company's existing manufacturing footprint. Intuitive Surgical is also considering potential changes to its supply chain. The tariffs that are actually implemented could also end up being significantly lower, creating less of a near-term gross margin headwind.

While tariffs could have a meaningful impact on Intuitive Surgical's business, the company is taking a wait-and-see approach in regard to pricing. Pricing changes are likely once the current situation is better understood though.

Financial Analysis

Intuitive Surgical generated 2.25 billion USD revenue in the first quarter, an increase of 19% YoY. Systems revenue increased 25%, driven by placements and higher system ASP (1.62 million USD, up 17% YoY). Recurring revenue grew 19% in Q1, driven by strong procedure growth.

Intuitive Surgical expects 15-17% procedure growth in 2025, up modestly from the prior quarter. The broader rollout of the da Vinci 5 system should lead to stronger revenue growth in the second half of the year. Analysts are currently only estimate 16% revenue growth in 2025, which appears too conservative.

The decline in gross margin has been driven by rising facilities costs associated with increased manufacturing capacity. Mix shift towards Ion and da Vinci 5 is also a headwind, as these products currently have lower margins. Intuitive plans on opening new manufacturing facilities in Germany and Bulgaria in 2025, and will expand instrument manufacturing capacity in Mexico, which will add to depreciation expenses and further weigh on gross margins. This will be at least partially offset by revenue growth though. Intuitive expects its pro forma gross margin to fall in the 65-66.5% range in 2025, although this doesn't take into account any tariff headwinds.

Pro forma operating expenses increased 12% YoY in Q1 driven by headcount (500 employee increase sequentially), facilities costs and legal fees. Pro forma operating expenses are expected to grow by 10-14% in 2025. Intuitive also expects 650-750 million USD CapEx in 2025, primarily related to facility construction. This likely means that margins will remain under pressure in 2025. Margins should begin to rebound in 2026 though, as growth investments moderate and da Vince 5 gains adoptions.

Intuitive ended the first quarter with 9.1 billion USD cash and investments. This, along with free cash flow, is being used as part of a 4 billion USD share repurchase plan. While this may seem impressive, it modest relative to Intuitive's 180 billion USD market capitalization.

Conclusion

The rollout of new products is supportive of growth but will continue to weigh margins in the near-term. Margins should begin to recover over the next few years though as Intuitive scales its da Vinci 5, SP and Ion user base. Looking beyond this, competition is set to increase. This will likely have minimal impact in Intuitive's more mature markets due to the preference of existing users for the da Vinci system. International markets will be needed to support growth longer term though, and lower priced competitors could be an issue in these markets. As a result, I tend to think that analysts are underestimating Intuitive's near-term prospects, but are more broadly correct about the company's longer term growth potential.

Increased investor confidence in Intuitive Surgical's competitive positioning and the robotic surgery opportunity are supportive of the company's valuation. Intuitive Surgical's business is maturing though, which will eventually lead to multiple compression. I believe the investors are too focused on the quality of Intuitive's business at the moment, and as a result, the company's valuation has risen to a level that will make future returns poor.

data.title: Beam Therapeutics: Progress Of BEAM-101 Presses Forward With H2 2025 Data Releases
data.description:
Beam Therapeutics (BEAM) is continuing to fire on all cylinders with respect to the development of its ex vivo hematopoietic stem cell transplant gene therapy known as BEAM-101. The reason why is because it just reported updated results from its phase 1/2 BEACON trial using this therapy to treat patients with sickle cell disease [SCD] with vaso-occlusive crisis [VOC]. The totality of the data includes 17 patients and is in line with previously reported data whereby there was induction of fetal hemoglobin > 60% and sickle hemoglobin [HbS] being reduced. What's even more important is the VOC that these patients have to deal with and this was not observed in any of them post engraftment.

Why do I believe that it is important to go over this biotech now though? Especially after it had already reported this data? The reason why is because the company is already in the process of looking to recruit another batch of patients and it believes that it could recruit up to 30 of them by mid-2025. Furthermore, it expects to release additional data from this phase 1/2 BEACON trial in the 2nd half of 2025. This is only the beginning in terms of the possible catalysts that investors should keep an eye on. That is because the company is also expected to release additional data from in vivo Lipid nanoparticle [LNP] gene therapy BEAM-302 for the treatment of patients with alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency [AATD] in the 2nd half of 2025. This company is also in the process of developing gene therapies for other rare disorders like Glycogen Storage Disease type [GSD1].

BEAM-101 Progress Continues With Further Recruitment To Reinforce Additional Findings

As I noted above, the company released an update on the use of it BEAM-101 gene therapy candidate to target patients with severe sickle cell disease [SCD] with vaso-occlusive crisis [VOC] in the ongoing phase 1/2 BEACON trial. Further updated data reinforced what was previously reported in terms of efficacy, which is a very good thing. Before going over this newly released clinical data, plus any catalysts to come about from this specific program, it is first important to understand what this disorder is and what the possible market opportunity for the company could end up being. Sickle Cell Disease [SCD] is a type of disorder characterized as the red blood cells [RBCs] of a patient becoming sickle-shaped or sticky. This is a major problem whereby these patients lack proper RBCs to use throughout their body for starters. Secondly, the sickle-shaped nature of these cells leads to a painful event of them getting stuck in small blood vessels, known as VOC. These painful episodes can last for days and/or weeks and is only the starting problem for these patients. That's because these SCD patients can also suffer from recurrent infections and also anemia [very dangerously low levels of RBCs in body]. The global sickle cell disease market is expected to reach $12.38 billion by 2032. This is a very large market opportunity, but the company is looking to help those with severe disease. Believe it or not, but this is still expected to be a large market opportunity. It is said that approximately 65% of patients diagnosed with SCD have the severe form of it [known as HbSS].

In order to see whether or not BEAM-101 is going to be effective in being able to treat patients with severe sickle-cell disease [SCD], it is in the process of running the ongoing phase 1/2 BEACON study. This particular early-stage trial has already recruited a total of 17 patient to date. It is important note immediately that this is not a placebo-controlled study. Instead, it is being run as a single-arm open-label type of study whereby these patients with SCD are given a single dose of intravenous [IV] BEAM-101 following myeloablative conditioning with busulfan. The primary endpoint itself is highly focused on the VOC crisis portion as an endpoint and it makes sense because this painful event can last for days or weeks. It is not something that these patients want to go through. With that being said the primary endpoint deployed for this phase 1/2 BEACON study was the change in annualized number of severe VOC events over a 6-month period. First and foremost, a problem with these patients is lack of fetal hemoglobin [HbF] being present. The gene therapy helped in this regard in that not only was there a >60% HbF induction achieved, but that Hemoglobin S [sickle hemoglobin] was reduced to less than 40%. While these are good findings, they don't get to the true matter at heart for these patients, which is that they experience VOC due to their disease. BEAM-101 definitely helped in this regard too, noting that patients that took this therapy had no VOC events at all. This reduced or non-existent VOC events means for patients no need for extended hospital stays or poor quality of life [QoL] being present for them.

This updated data was done with 17 patients who were treated with BEAM-101 to treat their severe SCD in this phase 1/2 BEACON trial. However, as I noted before, the company is looking to have completed recruitment of up to 30 patients with this disorder by mid-2025. Should this recruitment projection remain on track, then the biotech should be in the position of releasing data from this early-stage study in the 2nd half of 2025. This program is shaping up quite well indeed. The reason why I say that is because the FDA appears to have been impressed with the clinical data released as well, since it gave BEAM-101 Orphan Drug Designation [ODD]. This is crucial because such a status comes with a certain set of benefits like several years of market exclusivity, exemption of user fees and even tax credits.

Financials

According to the 10-Q SEC Filing, Beam Therapeutics had cash, cash equivalents and marketable securities of $1.2 billion as of March 31st of 2025. The reason for the cash on hand is because the company enacted a massive offering back in March of 2025. That is when it sold a total of 16,151,686 shares of its commons stock at an offering price of $28.48 per share. In total, it raised approximately $500 million from it. With that being said, I don't believe that there is any risk for near-term dilution. What makes me believe this to be the case? Well, it is because it stated in its Q1 of 2025 financial results and business update, it states that it believes it has enough cash on hand to fund its operations into 2028. It burns approximately $126.7 million of cash per quarter. This is broken down into $98.8 million in R&D expenses and then $27.9 million in G&A expenses.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Beam Therapeutics. The first risk to consider would be in terms of the development of BEAM-101 for the treatment of patients with severe sickle-cell disease [SCD] with VOC. The company is expected to provide updated data on a group of 30 patients with this disorder in the 2nd half of 2025. The risk here is that there is no assurance that the data to be released from this study is going to be positive for starters. Secondly, there is no assurance that the data to be released is going to allow the company to move its ex vivo HSC gene therapy forward into the next of studies needing to be completed.

The second risk to consider would be in terms of the company focusing on its other gene therapy known as BEAM-302 as an in vivo LNP therapy for the treatment of patients with alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency [AATD]. The goal is to complete dose escalation Part A and then begin dose escalation Part B of the phase 1/2 study using BEAM-302 to target this group of patients. The risk here is that updated data from this early-stage study is expected to be released at some point in the 2nd half of 2025. There is no assurance that the results to be released from this study are going to be positive. Nor, that the company will receive a green light from the FDA to begin the next trial for this particular program.

The third and final risk to consider would be in terms of the ongoing collaboration agreements that Beam had done with Apellis Pharmaceuticals (APLS) and Pfizer (PFE). The risk here is that there is no guarantee that development of in vivo LNP candidates for both of these partnerships will result in successful studies. Nor, that any candidate from such programs will ultimately be approved by regulators for marketing. Another risk to consider is that these collaboration agreements could end up being terminated. In that case, Beam could lose out on its ability to obtain milestone payments and/or royalties from such clinical candidates.

Conclusion

Beam Therapeutics continues to do well in terms of the development of its lead candidate BEAM-101 for the treatment of patients with severe SCD with VOC. The reason why is because the updated data from 17 patients pretty much reinforced what was seen earlier. For instance, the therapy was able to help patients achieve a HbF induction of >60%. Along with the fact that such patients given BEAM-101 had no VOC after engraftment. What remains now is to see if this trend continues with the next batch of data to be released from the phase 1/2 BEACON study. As I stated above, the goal is to add a total of 30 patients from this trial and then report updated data from this study in the 2nd half of 2025.

Besides this candidate, it will be important to track the progress of its in vivo LNP gene therapy candidate BEAM-302. This particular therapy is being developed to treat patients with alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency [AATD]. Updated data from this specific program is also expected in the latter part of this year, and it will further prove whether or not the company's gene editing technology is capable of helping these patients with these types of disorders. With additional updated results from the phase 1/2 BEACON trial using BEAM-101 to treat SCD patients with VOC expected in the 2nd half of 2025, plus the continued advancement of BEAM-302 to treat patients with AATD, I believe that investors could benefit from any potential gains made here.

data.title: New Legislation Creates A Trillion Dollar Opportunity In Circle
data.description:
Thesis Summary

Congress just passed the GENIUS Act, laying the groundwork for stablecoin regulation and implementation.

Circle (CRCL), which recently went public and is the company behind USDC (USDC-USD), one of the most dominant stablecoins, could be a significant winner from this legislation.

Stablecoins could potentially revolutionise payments, and even a small slice of this large market could be worth trillions.

Though the stock has already rallied a lot since its IPO, this one is worth holding for the long term, although there are meaningful risks investors should consider

I rate CRCL a Buy, and suggest getting some exposure while leaving plenty of dry powder to add to the dips.

The GENIUS Act

The United States Senate has just passed the GENIUS Act, which means that dollar-backed stablecoins like USDC will not operate under federal supervision.

The GENIUS Act lays out the basic rules for stablecoins, like maintaining full 1:1 reserves and keeping transparent bookkeeping.

This is something USDC was already doing, unlike Tether (USDT-USD).

This new bill will now move over to the House, where it may also be merged with other crypto legislation like the STABLE Act.

It is likely that the bill will become law sometime before August.

Why Stablecoins Matter

While a lot of people may see stablecoins as just another crypto-adjacent fad, nothing could be further from the truth.

Stablecoins represent the net evolution of fiat money, which is very different from crypto or Bitcoin.

Stablecoins offer significant potential advantages over today’s digital money.

  • Instant settlement

  • No intermediaries

  • Near-zero fees

  • No chargebacks

  • Easy Programmability

And most importantly, no barriers to entry.

Through USDC, almost anyone in the world can get instant access to the USD, Treasuries and any app, product or investment that can be linked to it.

This has already been proven in many ways, with USDC and Tether operating for years now, and various DeFi products offering yield on these coins.

The next big step will be for stablecoins to offer a better alternative to payment processors and credit cards.

This is likely why, while Circle rallied yesterday, we saw Mastercard (MA) and Visa (V) take a big hit.

How Circle Fits In

Circle is by some measures the most dominant player in the Stablecoin market. In terms of market share, it holds 26%, second to Tether.

However, it is the largest stablecoin in terms of transfer volume, accounting for around 58% of the market.

Circle has issues around $60 billion in USDC, and it generates income by generating yield on the Treasuries that back up the stablecoin.

Back in 2024, the company generated around $1.5 billion in interest on a $60 billion supply of USDC.

However, we can see that a big chunk of this went to distribution and transaction costs. A big chunk of this is due to an agreement with Coinbase (COIN), which receives 50% of the revenues USDC generates. More on that later.

Growth Forecast: Trillion Dollar Opportunity

According to a report from Standard Chartered and Zodia Markets, stablecoins represent around 1% of the total US Money Supply and FX transactions.

The report also speculates that this could grow to around 10% of the total supply.

If this can happen, which seems reasonable based on all the advantages of stablecoins and USDC can reach a dominant market share, say 50%, which make sense if we look at transaction volume, and the fact that USDC is the only real large “legitimate” player, since USDT does not follow the current regulations, then this is a possible forecast for revenues.

By 2035, USDC could have over $1 trillion in circulation, with revenues reaching around $26 billion, which implies an effective return rate of 2.5%. This also implies a CAGR of nearly 30% over the next decade.

This would put Circle in the revenue ballpark of Visa, which commands a $600 billion valuation.

Risks

With a look at the table above, Circle looks like a screaming buy, but there are at least three very important risk factors to consider

Coinbase Partnership

As it stands right now, 50% of Circle’s revenues go to Coinbase. This agreement seems to be open-ended, but it could be renegotiated.

This deal made sense initially, as Coinbase was a key strategic partner, but this could change as the balance of power shifts.

The deal will automatically renew in August 2026, but Circle could look to renegotiate the terms then.

Competition

USDT is also a big player that could still de-throne USDC and even take market share. And where there’s money to be made, there will be plenty of new competitors.

I like the fact that USDC is already established and has regulatory compliance though.

In some ways, I see USDT and USDC as the Visa and Mastercard of the future.

Yields

Most of USDC’s business, right now, relies on receiving interest from yields. As yields come down, earnings will decrease.

Whereas a company like Visa commands a 70% EBITDA margin, Circle’s margins are much smaller once we account for costs and the revenue share.

Arguably, a quick move down in yields in the next year could even make the company unprofitable again.

Final Thoughts

The stablecoin market is on the verge of making a major disruption to money and finance. Not only for FX transactions, but potentially for so much more, including potentially the whole payment market, worth over $5 trillion by 2030.

USDC is very well positioned to benefit, but the valuation is already very high for a company that has to pay out half its revenue to Coinbase and is very dependent on yields.

Nonetheless, I want some exposure to this company, and will be looking to buy further as the stock dips.

data.title: Dutch Bros Inc.: Valuation Has Already Priced In Near-Term Upside
data.description:
Investment overview

I give a hold rating for Dutch Bros Inc. (NYSE:BROS). I believe the long-term growth algorithm remains intact, with clear drivers from unit expansion, digital adoption, and food innovation. That said, I see the near-term upside as limited given the current valuation already prices in much of the positives.

Business description

BROS is a drive-thru coffee chain that was founded in 1992 in Oregon. Since then, BROS has scaled to ~1,000 shops across 18 US states as of 1Q25. Its business model is centered on drive-thru-only locations with quick service and a menu that includes coffee, energy drinks, teas, etc. A noteworthy aspect of BROS' operating model is its internal promotion model, wherein new shops are typically led by longtime employees, averaging seven years of tenure.

Idiosyncratic Growth Drivers Provide High Visibility

BROS has multiple company-specific growth drivers that, in my opinion, offer clear upside regardless of macroeconomic conditions. The primary growth driver here is the highly visible unit expansion strategy. Management has outlined a long-term TAM of 7,000 stores in the US, with a medium-term goal of reaching 2,029 shops by 2029. Doing the math implies mid-teens unit growth CAGR, which, coupled with historical same-store-sales growth [SSSG], translates into ~20% revenue CAGR. These targets are not aggressive at all if you consider that Starbucks (SBUX) has over 16,000 locations in the US alone. The quality of these new units gives even more confidence. Systemwide annualized new unit volumes have hit $2 million (was $1.95 million on a blended basis in 1Q25), and this shows that BROS has been realizing improved productivity from new stores. Hence, not only is there a clear line of sight to opening new stores, these new stores are also performing better, which means SSSG could also accelerate.

Aside from unit growth, digital and menu innovation are also key growth drivers. The Mobile Order & Pay [MOP] rollout is a good example. MOP was introduced nationwide in 4Q24, and it already represented 11% of all transactions by the end of 1Q25 (up from 8% in 4Q24). My belief is this will drive up order frequency, especially in the morning, where speed and convenience matter most. Sound familiar? It should because this is a playbook from SBUX where it leveraged a mobile app to drive up order frequency. Beyond this increase in order frequency, it also has a very positive impact on store efficiency as it shifts some demand to walk-up windows (MOP collection is at the walk-up window). This would lift some burden off the heavy drive-thru window.

Looking ahead, the way I see it, the next major growth lever is an expanded food menu. BROS is already testing this out. The initiative, currently in 32-store test, is focused on eight simple items, including four hot food SKUs. I like that management started small, as this allows them to be flexible in trying out more variety of food products (if need be), while containing any potential damages (this initiative could fail horribly, and it's only a few stores that gets hit). I also support the idea behind launching food because it can lift beverage sales by driving morning traffic. While food has a lower gross margin than drinks, the right way to think about this is how it will flow down to the bottom line. Remember that a bunch of BROS' operating costs are fixed (land/rental and labor). Higher store utilization means higher operating leverage. Suppose this initiative works and picks off—a broader rollout is targeted for 2026—this food strategy provides another leg to SSSG.

Margin Headwinds from Costs and Tariffs

Margin pressure is a problem to note, particularly from commodity and labor inflation. On the COGS front, elevated coffee bean prices themselves are already a pain, and if we top it off with the fact that coffee, which represents most of BROS's international sourcing exposure, is vulnerable to a proposed 10% tariff on imports from countries like Brazil, Colombia, and El Salvador, near-term profitability could really get hurt. The good news is that management has already set the expectations right. They have factored the tariff impact into their FY25 outlook, now expecting ~110 bps of net COGS pressure this year. This guide has high reliability since BROS has already locked in the majority of its coffee prices for the remainder of the year.

Regarding labor, it is also a problem to watch out for. In 1Q25, labor costs grew 100 bps y/y as a percentage of company-operated shop revenue. driven both by mandated wage hikes (the California minimum wage increase in April 2024) and voluntary wage hikes aimed to better incentivize store-level managers. Management has also made another round of compensation enhancements in April 2025. While these investments are strategically sound and go a long way to incentivize workers and build a positive culture, they come at a cost. The offsetting factor is that BROS can outgrow this fixed cost growth by growing topline much faster. But if topline fails to grow as fast, BROS could see its profit stay pressured for longer than expected.

Valuation – the stock is not cheap

Fundamentally, I lean towards positive for BROS. I believe it is able to continue to grow top line at low-20% for the foreseeable future, and margin headwinds shouldn’t be an issue. However, valuation is the problem. I don’t think the stock is cheap. BROS already trades at 40x forward EBITDA, which can be argued as a fair multiple since Wingstop (WING) trades at 41x with ~20% revenue growth expectation. This also means that, at 40x, BROS is probably already pricing in the near-term upsides, which, indeed, as per the model, the upside from here is not attractive. I assumed 22% revenue growth through FY26 with a 17% adj. EBITDA margin in FY26, which equates to adj. EBITDA of $324 million. Attaching 40x here translates to a price target of $79, which is just ~10% higher than where it trades today.

Conclusion

I give a hold rating for BROS. There’s a lot to like fundamentally, especially the visible topline growth and solid execution. However, I don’t see a reason to own this stock since the stock is already trading at ~40x forward EBITDA, which is where peers with similar growth profiles trade. I think it makes more sense for investors to wait for a better entry.

data.title: Highwoods Properties: FFO Set To Begin Recovery From 2021-2024 Slump
data.description:
Introduction

So far in 2025, Highwoods Properties (NYSE:HIW) has outperformed the Vanguard Real Estate Index Fund ETF (VNQ), delivering a total return of ~8%:

Even after the strong run, the REIT is not prohibitively expensive, trading at a 9.4x FFO multiple and an 8.2% market-implied cap rate before administrative overhead. Taking into account my outlook for higher FFO starting in 2026 (driven by higher occupancy and lower interest rates), I think that a Buy rating is justified for HIW.

Company Overview

You can access all Highwoods Properties results on its website here. Highwoods Properties is an office REIT focused almost exclusively on the Sunbelt region, which accounts for over 95% of the company's net operating income [NOI]. Raleigh is the largest single market for the company (23.3% of NOI), followed by Nashville (20.1%), Atlanta (15.1%), and Charlotte (11.5%):

The tenant base is quite diversified, with no single tenant accounting for more than 4% of revenue. From a sector perspective, Finance and Banking accounts for 19% of annualized revenue, followed by Legal and Accounting Services (16%), and Insurance at 11%:

The company's enterprise value stands at $6.9 billion, of which 51% is funded with common equity, 48.6% with net debt, leaving just 0.4% for preferred shares.

Q1 2025 Results Overview

Highwoods Properties reported Q1 2025 FFO of $0.83/share, down 7% Y/Y and 2% Q/Q. The primary driver behind the decline in FFO was lower occupancy, down 3% Y/Y to 85.5%, which is still a decent level for an office REIT given the challenging operating environment.

Same property cash NOI decreased 3.3%, largely driven by the occupancy decline. On a more encouraging note, post-quarter end, the company highlighted that it had achieved 300,000 square feet of new leases as of June 2, 2025. I estimate that this should be enough to boost occupancy by 1% once leases commence.

Increased 2025 Outlook

Given the robust leasing momentum in Q2 2025, the company increased its FFO outlook to $3.31-3.47/share in 2025. This implies that the REIT should be able to generate FFO of $0.85/share for the remainder of the year, in essence putting an end to the period of FFO decline observed all the way back to 2021 when quarterly FFO peaked at $1.06/share in Q4 2021:

Debt Overview

The company's debt is well-laddered, with maturities picking up in 2027-2029:

The weighted average interest rate stands at just 4.46%, largely driven by a small proportion of floating rate debt (only 14.9% of all debt), which carries an interest rate of 5.25-5.35%. Indeed, interest on the company's revolving credit facility is based on the secured overnight financing rate [SOFR] plus 0.9%, which is quite attractive for an office REIT.

As such, given market expectations for roughly 0.75% in Fed rate cuts later in 2025 and early 2026, we can reasonably assume that Highwoods Properties will see an annual $0.03/share FFO boost from lower interest rates in 2026 and going into 2027. Indeed, I anticipate that financing costs will decline until 2030-2031 when legacy debt carrying low rates of 2.65-3.24% needs to be refinanced.

Company Valuation

I estimate that Highwoods Properties' current FFO annual run-rate stands at about $3.40/share, or $373 million. What is more, the company is paying roughly $146 million in annual interest (which is set to decline to about $142 million given the outlook for lower Fed interest rates) and $0.6 million in preferred share dividends.

Taking the combined company cash flows of ~$520 million and dividing them by the $6.9 billion enterprise value, we see that the market implied cap rate stands at ~7.6%. As the $520 million already incorporates $40 million in general and administrative expenses, the market-implied cap rate from an acquirer's perspective stands at ~8.2%.

Using my $3.40/share FFO run-rate estimate, we see that the shares trade at a FFO multiple of 9.4x, certainly not the cheapest among office REITs, but overall attractive given the robust occupancy developments and modest leverage (which is decreasing thanks to a conservative payout ratio of 59%)

All in all, even after the outperformance so far in 2025, I think Highwoods Properties is worth a Buy rating as a stabilizing occupancy and a marginal boost from lower interest expenses should help FFO return to growth as early as 2026.

Risks

The principal risk facing investors is that occupancy does not stabilize at current levels, potentially falling below 80%. This will likely result in lower FFO, making the current 9.4x multiple justified. Highwoods Properties' strategy to mitigate this risk lies in focusing on attractive Sun Belt locations, which have recently seen strong population and employment growth.

Of course, previous trends may not continue into the future as President Trump's policy on restricting immigration could reverse a 50-year-old trend of net immigration to the United States.

The other key risk to mention is that the Fed may not cut interest rates as fast as markets expect. Highwoods Properties has limited exposure to floating-rate debt, but unless the Fed cuts interest rates by 2030-2031, the REIT's interest costs will likely increase.

Conclusion

Highwoods Properties delivered FFO of $0.83/share in Q1 2025, continuing a trend of declines going back to 2021. The two principal drivers behind the FFO drop over the past four years - lower occupancy and higher interest costs - are likely to improve in the years ahead.

The company has managed to achieve robust leasing activity in Q2 2025, while markets price in easing by the Fed later in 2025 and going into 2026. This should allow FFO to return to growth starting in 2026.

While certainly not the cheapest office REIT, Highwoods Properties trades at 9.4x its FFO run-rate of about $3.40/share, representing a market-implied cap rate of 8.2% before administrative expenses. Considering the improving outlook for FFO, I think that this valuation is quite attractive, ranking HIW a Buy.

Investors should consider risks stemming from further declines in occupancy and no interest rate cuts by the Fed, which may dent the increase in FFO I expect for 2026.

Thank you for reading.

data.title: Tyson Foods: Navigating Beef Challenges While Betting On Chicken And Automation
data.description:
Walking Through the Cautious Upside While Navigating the Contrarian Signals

We initiate on Tyson Foods, Inc. (NYSE:TSN) at Hold/$47 PT. Tyson Foods, Inc. is a global protein processor and marketer of beef, pork, chicken, and prepared foods for retail, foodservice, and industrial customers. Underneath the surface of the large-cap operator lies the legacy volatility in Beef and early-stage perks of the transformation in Chicken and Branded Foods.

Our estimate captures FY25 diluted EPS of $3.60 (+60% YY) / -6% N-Cons as we back in the full -$200mn/yr Beef segment loss and additional incremental Chicken-brand investments already highlighted by management that are often understood from reported N-benchmarks. While these near-term headwinds may not clear the air quickly, grain tailwinds and production ramp should push Chicken AOI toward $1.25bn by FY26 and give TSN headroom as the cycle reverts. Simultaneously, we see the Prepared Foods margin slowly grind higher through decent execution, but value and strategies at large accounts like WMT (which accounts for 18.4% of revenue) keep us cautious.

With this backdrop, we set our target multiple at 11.5x on FY26E EPS, a slight premium to peers, but a deep discount to the current 21.1x, reflecting the credibility of the earnings instance, but tempered by cyclicality and outsized capex with automation and the beef rebuild. Execution is the key variable in our view, particularly in the ability to normalize beef and the possibility of incremental cost inflation. Net-net, with shares priced well above our derived fair value on muted risk-reward, we look to observe until the margin recovery story gets institutional endorsement and wait and re-evaluate.

Automation Drives Margins Higher

Our view of Tyson Foods’ ongoing commitment to operational execution and automation is a yet-untapped margin catalyst that is not sufficiently reflected in the R/R of the stock, in our view. More than $1.3bn in automation investment committed to date, paired with a stated ambition to achieve $1bn in recurring productivity savings, reflects an execution record that should set the company apart from more capital-constrained peers.

Importantly, Tyson’s automation efforts are no longer project pilots, and the case is being made on core line item impact: CEO Donnie King has highlighted $400mn in targeted automation savings on chicken deboning, while the new $300mn Virginia plant’s utilization of automatic case packing, x-ray and visual sensors point to more consistent, lower labor-exposure production. More importantly, they’re not soft targets: with evidence coming to bear in 2024 including the operating margin rebound to 2.6% and chicken hitting 8.6% in the latest quarter – both multi-year highs – the underlying productivity improvements are certainly attainable.

Additionally, management comments have placed emphasis on maintaining CapEx near depreciation levels, signaling a shift from catch-up to disciplined management of assets and a focus on targeted investments. While some risk remains on external factors, such as inflationary labor trends, we believe Tyson’s commitment to automation and operational improvements is already starting to mitigate these risks and create structural, defensible advantages. The upside to our thesis requires visibility into these savings more than offsetting beef headwinds and translating into FCF conversion. In the interim, automation is an operational tailwind that underpins what we view as the quality of the earnings recovery.

Beef Margin Compression

We find structural headwinds facing Tyson’s beef segment non-transitory and, on all evidence to date, remain a material drag on consolidated performance. Beef reported $5.0bn of quarterly sales (+1% y/y) with operating losses widening to $96.5mn vs. a loss of $34mn in the prior corresponding period. Of equal or greater relevance, we note that segment margins compressed to 1.3% as live cattle prices advanced 12.8%, materially ahead of retail protein pricing and leaving Tyson unable to pass through cost pressures. We note that the protein cycle has decisively turned against beef packers with retail pass-through burn-off eroding demand fundamentals.

We believe that the macro market dynamic of elevated shelf prices has shifted consumer demand towards lower-cost proteins, materially impairing Tyson’s pricing power and rendering inventory planning difficult, evidenced by management commentary and market data suggestive of demand destruction. Concurrently, we believe structural trade headwinds in the form of tariffs levied on US beef exports into Canada and Japan are imposing an additional drag on the beef protein mix given the impairment of market access with resolution not in sight and industry forecasts for continuing pressure into 2026. Volatile feed and energy input prices, including a +15% YTD increase in corn prices, further magnify margin risk and create volatility in procurement and production planning.

In our view, the confluence of elevated cattle costs and weak demand elasticity against a backdrop of international trade headwinds create a structural profitability ceiling for Tyson’s beef operations. We see little risk of a return to historical margin averages pre-2026 and acknowledge limited further group-level upside. We, therefore, await further information and maintain our Hold rating and $47 PT.

Tyson FY25–26 Earnings Growth Outlook: Below Consensus but Bracketing the Beef Cycle Downside

We model Tyson FY25E EPS of $3.60 (+60% y/y) and FY26E of $4.10 (+13.9% y/y), 6% below the street for FY25 and broadly inline for FY26 (+0.5%). Our less constructive FY25 outlook owes to our conservative treatment of the beef segment, where we embed a full-year $200mn loss vs. the street's break-even view and management's recognition of "the most challenging market conditions we've ever seen."

Source: FMP FY25 FY26
Estimate # Analysts Estimate # Analysts
Revenue $54.14B 6 $55.18B 8
EBITDA $4.37B 6 $4.45B 8
Net Income $1.37B 6 $1.42B 8
EPS $3.83 6 $4.08 7
SG&A Expenses $2.45B 6 $2.50B 8

Additionally, we build in $100mn of incremental chicken brand investment ahead of the 2H25 ramp-up, which, we believe, is underappreciated by consensus. Offsetting headwinds, we model flat-to-mildly improving AOI in Chicken ($1.15bn FY25E, $1.25bn FY26E) as grain input costs moderate and the value-added mix expands. Prepared Foods' margins continue to improve on sustained efficiencies in production and labor, though risk into Walmart (18.4% of sales) pricing remains a watch point.

Our FY26E estimate assumes additional savings in logistics and a muted beef rebound as herd rebuild activity begins, though we are cautious about a pronounced rebound in the segment given the dynamics around feedstock and cattle prices. Year-to-year, we think our modeling approaches the likely boundary of outcomes on current visibility, given the ongoing volatility in key inputs and export tariffs.

Valuation

Our $47 PT is based on a forward P/E of 11.5x our FY26E EPS of $4.10, with an implied downside of -13% from current levels, presenting a risk-averse outlook.

We find 11.5x fair (if not at a slight premium) to its long-term median (11.2x) as well as peer group (10.7x), and substantially below its current rich 21.1x, which is in the upper-90th percentile of both its own long-term and the sector's.

We see a slight premium to peers validated by its credible double-digit earnings growth runway (FY26E +13.9%), as it translates improvements in chicken and prepared foods. Beef headwinds and financing of modernization are outside-in considerations on the thesis in the near term.

EBITDA margins and grain cost tailwinds also persuade outperformance, while beef volatility and high customer concentration (WMT 18.4% of revenue) are underpinned by valuation.

We believe our PT is appropriately balanced given the current fundamental backdrop and cyclical risks and maintain a Hold rating as they work to establish a more sustainable margin recovery story.

Downside Risks to a Hold Rating

Our Hold call on TSN could prove overly conservative if a collection of the following key factors play out more favorably than modeled in our valuation framework:

(1) Tyson's operational improvement initiatives bear fruit, potentially delivering accelerated leverage to the chicken AOI profile and prepared foods segment margins if cost recapture and execution on automation meaningfully exceed current expectations, particularly if grain costs prove to be more benign and incremental margin expansion persists across the segments, supporting management's optimistic AOI commentary and leading to more upwards revisions to consensus expectations;

(2) Beef segment recovery accelerates faster than modeled, for example with cattle herd rebuilding surprising to the upside or with demand trends for beef recovering more quickly than anticipated, alleviating the margin pressure that has lingered in the segment;

(3) Strategic investments in automation, branded innovation, and operational discipline enable more meaningful cash conversion or margin stability than expected, even in the face of ongoing protein market volatility, potentially triggering a meaningful rerating if the street becomes more comfortable with TSN's EPS resilience;

(4) Other key monitorables that could drive a re-rating include (A) accelerating progress towards chicken AOI run-rate, (B) tangible progress on prepared foods margin expansion, and (C) any signs of segment inflection towards profitability in Beef ahead of consensus expectations, which could collectively underpin the sensitivity of our valuation framework.

Conclusion

We see TSN as a Hold as its risk-reward offset by near-term beef volatility and the disproportional hurdle remaining to show margin recovery despite encouraging chicken and prepared Foods' improvement. Our conviction lies in the below-consensus growth discipline and a valuation that prices in much of the recovery, leaving limited upside to surprise until outsized execution beats. Patience remains for clear operational leverage and margin normalization signals–especially on the beef & branded foods' side—before adopting a higher conviction view.

data.title: Don't Buy The S&P 500, Buy This Sector Instead
data.description:
Dear readers,

In April, amidst pretty extreme tariff fears, I published an article on my largest holding - the S&P 500 (NYSEARCA:SPY), urging investors to buy into the crash.

At the time, many were bearish and calling for an even deeper sell-off, as evident from comments I received on my last article:

- The market has not crashed. It’s barely in correction territory ... There is more downside from here unless Trump pulls a rabbit out of his hat.

- Woa! We're just at the end of the first inning. Today's rally has already faded and anyone who thinks that this is a buying opportunity, well I have a bridge in Brooklyn to sell you.

- SP500 (is going) to 2795.

This doesn't come as a surprise.

People get emotional, especially when fear sets in.

And there was, indeed, a lot of fear at the time, as (i) the volatility index (VIX) spiked to 61, a level only seen a handful of times in recent history, and (ii) the fear and greed index fell to just 3 (out of 100), the lowest level since Covid.

As investors, however, our job is to try to ignore those emotions. We all know the saying "buy when there's blood in the streets", yet few were able to pull the trigger in April.

In retrospect, my call has paid off big time, with an RoR of 15% realized over just two months.

But the easy money has been made and the S&P 500 is, in my opinion, no longer an intriguing buy.

Below I explain why and provide an alternative sector which, I believe, is positioned to outperform the broad market index.

Downgrading the S&P 500 to a HOLD

A lot has changed since April.

On the tariff front, the White House has significantly toned down their voice since April, making it seem that their aggressive initial stance was indeed only a negotiation tactic.

Tariffs have effectively been put on pause for 90 days, or until July 9. A date which is quickly approaching.

The U.S. has reached a couple of trade deals so far, most importantly with China reducing the proposed tariff from 145% to 30%. The market has seen the deal with China as a win, but we should note that 30% is still a very high tariff and that there are still no deals made with the majority of other countries (incl. important trading partners such as the EU). My expectation is that we are not out of the woods yet, as the V-shaped market recovery would have you believe, as trade deals will likely take longer to negotiate than expected, likely needing another extension after July 9.

This is a major concern for inflation, as summarized by the Fed Chair Jay Powell:

Tariffs have already raised prices on some goods – especially electronics – and inflation could spike in the coming months as higher import taxes make their way through the system.

Inflation remains relatively stable, but did tick up from 2.3% in April to 2.4% in May. This, however, was primarily a result of shelter, which increased by 0.3% month-over-month (or 3.6% YoY). Going forward, however, there is real concern that inflation may reignite if tariff talks are not resolved.

While inflation is not yet showing strong signs of tariffs, GDP growth is!

Notably, GDP growth declined from 2.4% in Q4 2024 to negative 0.3% in Q1 2024. This sudden drop was almost entirely caused by a surge in imports as companies and individuals scrambled to buy foreign goods before tariffs get implemented. Imports deducted a full 5% from GDP growth in Q1. Part of imports ended up as inventories, partially offsetting the hit to GDP with a positive addition of 2.3%, but still, the overall effect was decisively negative.

Negative GDP growth is not expected to last, with current consensus Q2 growth at 1.5%. But I do think it's likely that consumers and companies have pulled forward a lot of demand, which likely inflated Q1 earnings and will cause a drag on earnings over the following quarters.

In the meantime, the Fed continues to sit on its hands and is keeping interest rates unchanged in relatively restrictive territory of 4.25-4.50% as they see:

a modestly stagflationary picture of the economy, with growth in 2025 slowing to 1.4%, unemployment rising to 4.5%, and inflation ending the year at 3%, well above the current level.

In summary, the economic situation seems worse than it was prior to the tariff crash, yet the stock market has recovered nearly all of the losses and trades near all-time highs. This, in my opinion, creates relatively poor risk-reward to add new money into the market.

At the bottom of the crash, valuation was starting to feel more reasonable at 18.7x forward earnings. But after the recovery, the market once again trades at a near all-time high valuation of 22.4x forward earnings. That's almost two standard deviations away from the 20-year mean, and therefore indicative of poor forward returns over the next 5–10 years.

I'm a long-term investor and never sell my SPY position, despite a gloomy macroeconomic picture. But I try to allocate new money in a sensible way.

Back in April, I thought it made a lot of sense to add to my SPY position.

Today, I see the S&P 500 as a HOLD and will rather allocate new funds elsewhere.

Why semiconductors will outperform

To understand which sectors may dominate going forward, it's helpful to look at history.

Currently, the S&P 500 is dominated by software companies, which account for 27% of the overall index. But this wasn't always the case. Just two decades ago, in 2002, software barely accounted for 5%.

So why has software dominated so much in the twenty-first century?

I believe it was a result of two moat characteristics.

Firstly, as the internet became more widely adopted, companies naturally needed to invest in their digital infrastructure. This created a very high demand for software solutions and resulted in a fast growth environment for software companies.

Secondly, software companies were able to achieve very high margins because of high barriers to entry. The barriers to entry were high primarily because:

  1. twenty years ago, there were very few people capable of building software, resulting in a labor shortage,
  2. and software ran on physical servers (rather than cloud) which meant very high CAPEX.

But the world has changed, and today software can be created very cheaply with the help of AI, programmers are plentiful, and the cloud provides vast amounts of computing power for a fraction of the price. Simply put, the two moat characteristics that helped software get to where it is today are gone.

At the same time, the semiconductor industry currently has the very same characteristics. We're seeing unprecedented demand for AI-chips, margins are very high, and it seems quite unlikely that new competition could ever threaten the big players such as Nvidia (NVDA) or Broadcom (AVGO).

Because of this, I believe that it is likely that semiconductors will eat up market share from software over time.

The industry has already doubled since 2022 and currently has a 12% weight in the S&P 500. But with the share of software stocks likely to tumble, I believe there's potential for the share of semiconductor stocks to double once more.

At 24% of the overall index, the share of semiconductor stocks would still be below (i) that of software today - 27%, (ii) that of the energy sector in the 1980s - 40%, and (iii) that of dot-com stocks in early 2000 - 40%.

For this reason, I recommend investors look into semiconductor ETFs and consider adding those to their SPY exposure. Personally, I like the iShares Semiconductor ETF (SOXX) and VanEck Semiconductor ETM (SMH).

data.title: The Stagflation Economy
data.description:
Fed makes few changes and reveals huge risks

The Fed policy meeting (Federal Reserve) in June was considered uneventful, as no action was expected, and no action was taken. The markets did not react. The Fed did issue its new SEPS (Summary of Economic Projections- these are the outlooks offered by Federal Open Market Committee {FOMC} members) and "Dots" (this refers to the outlook by members for levels of the Federal funds rate at year-end). You can be excused if you viewed these reports as the release of the Fed's forecasts because it is not exactly that even though it really looks like that. The Fed itself has a fluctuating relationship with the SEPS and Dots and refers to them differently at different times. Suffice it to say that these are pooled 'forecasts' made in isolation by FOMC members under certain constraints. No attempt is made to reconcile them or the assumptions each member has made to under pin his or her SEP contribution or dot. That is a complication in referring to what the average, range, or even median of these SEPS or even the dots really means. While some look at the median dot as expressing the Fed's desire for/or expectation for the Fed fund rate (at year-end) in face the median dot is the median of all 19 committee members and not all members vote on policy (the FOMC has 12 members at any one time if it is fully staffed). That makes the meaning of the median dot even more elusive.

The Fed continues to do things like that that look like one thing but really are not that thing, and then it wonders why it has such a hard time with communications. The SEPS and Dots are a good example of this.

Fed Chairman Powell (Chair Powell) talks about how the Fed is in a good place with options open. He says inflation expectations are anchored. Yet, the University of Michigan Consumer survey shows spiking inflation expectations for the year ahead, as well as for five years ahead. And in the Fed’s own SEPS released on Wednesday, show an expectation that inflation will continue to overshoot its target for the sixth year in a row as the Fed plans further but measured rate cuts. What good is a target you do not try to hit?

Few people are critical of this, and I want to know why. If the central bank has a target for inflation of 2%, how can it go four years in a row missing it then release an outlook that includes interest rates cuts and still fails to reach its inflation target over the next two years? How does that work? Why does a central bank that acts that way think it has credibility?

So, I am concerned about what this policy by the Fed means. This is not just a riff off a Fed meeting whose outcome I did not like. This new set of data from the Fed shows us how little-committed the Fed is to a target that Chair Powell continues to tell us is the target of Fed policy – subject to the second leg of the Fed’s dual mandate, of course, the employment situation usually expresses as the rate of unemployment.

During Covid the Fed let itself get behind the inflation curve and told itself a story that inflation was temporary. Some economists argued that when there is a supply shock the Fed should not react to it. The idea is that higher rates can quell excess demand and bring inflation down. But if demand is only steady and a shock lowers supply, a rate hike will do nothing to increase supply.

But as we saw, that did not work. Instead, inflation spread not just to the afflicted goods sector but across the services sector and the Fed wound up chasing rising inflation with exceptional rate hikes. Is the Fed ready to do that again?

Déjà vu

The Fed currently has a prepared policy that is 'biased’ to cut rates with inflation still above target and with more ‘temporary’ price pressures on the horizon from tariffs and oil prices amid other risks. But, of course, if the Fed misplays policy, these temporary pressures on prices could become inflation. And apart from these two risks, there is also a situation of looming fiscal deficits.

The Fed needs to be wary of what is in train and how its current policy tilt plays into it. Yet, it does not look one bit like the policy it is preparing to unfold would be the preferred policy of a true inflation hating central banker. What the Fed plans is a policy that is not particularly worried about inflation that is excessive but only moderate and while more risks lurk, the Fed sees them as limited and temporary - unless, of course, they are not. All this is too cavalier for a central bank to think it has credibility.

The stagflation economy

The risk here is a rise of inflation and the development of stagflation. Stagflation is a condition where inflation is too-high and growth is too-weak at the same time, and that the condition endures. Stagflation morphs quite readily from a failed policy objective of 'soft-landing.' The Fed has been downplaying its inflation misses and yet compulsive about keeping unemployment low since after Covid struck. This policy tilt could do only one thing: fail to lower inflation and overheat the economy. While not raising rates more and seeing inflation get stuck too high, the Fed has continued to spin a tale of more rate cuts to come and maintained that its policy - as it is - is tight enough to get inflation falling, even though that is not happening. And new risks lie ahead. Since the Fed has tried to juggle the dual mandate misses the unemployment rate at 4.2% is up from its very temporary and very low, (multi-decade low), of 3.4%. Inflation is down from its peak (CPI Yr/Yr that briefly had gotten over 9%) and the Fed’s own PCE gauge now shows core inflation still over target and the Fed’s outlook sees it rising more as tariff effects kick in. Yet, the Fed sees rate cuts over its policy horizon. Tariffs and rising oil prices also have the effect of damaging growth, so they could also slow growth and raise the unemployment rate, further cementing the outlook for stagflation. And with huge budget deficits already spiking inflation expectations -at least in some surveys - the case for inflation and stagflation is an easy one to make from here. It does not take imagination.

A Fed that's not vigilant makes inflation virulent

I have been writing various reports on Seeking Alpha, warning of the risks of a soft-landing policy, for some time (More on Soft-Landings... Soft-landing-risk). I have noted in these articles, as well as in my own substack writings, that soft landings simply never work -Never Work! The central bank can mute the business cycle if that is what it seeks. But a policy of soft-landing has never taken a rising rate of inflation kept the unemployment rate from rising (or from rising by much) and ALSO returned inflation to the spot it was before inflation began to accelerate. This past quasi-soft-landing did get unemployment back to a low point after Covid; inflation once roared over 9% in CPI terms; it is now between 2.5% and 3% - clear progress. But it has not gotten back to 2% on the core PCE. And now further acceleration is looming.

How does the Fed expect markets to react? Does it think markets will be content to see Fed rates sit unchanged as inflation rises? Why would they do that after seeing what happened in 2021, 2022, and 2023? Are markets without memory? Or does the Fed think it has gained some credibility with markets after missing its target for four years running? And quite apart from tariffs and oil prices, what about those fiscal deficits?

Through the looking glass? The Fed is living in a world I do not recognize. As a central bank, I do not recognize the Fed’s priorities. As a central bank that has made policy mistakes, I see no evidence of caution or of learning from past mistakes. The Fed seems willing to take the same risks again that it played badly a few years ago. And as for its belief or perception of anchored inflation expectations, I do not begin to get that at all.

Inflation not interest rates is the root of all evil

Meanwhile, the Fed calls its policy 'mildly restrictive.' Will it still be so when inflation rises again? Moreover, is anyone aware that the Fed’s 'higher-for-longer' policy (soft-landing) has raised debt financing costs? The only way to reduce those costs is to get rates lower. But the solution is not to cut Fed funds. The solution may be to raise Fed funds! Because only getting inflation in order will truly allow interest rates to fall and stay lower. All the Fed's "keeping of rates lower than in the past to orchestrate a soft-landing and protect against unemployment rising" has caused an extended inflation problem and higher interest rates - that includes mortgage rates - that’s right folks! All of this is the result of the choice the Fed has made to run policy. To protect unemployment, the Fed has pushed higher rates onto the economy, affecting fiscal financing and mortgage financing and all other borrowing rates. And that is on top of raising the risk that we wind up with stagflation. This was a policy choice, and you can see now -in retrospect - that it pushed disequilibrium from the labor market into the capital markets by leaving inflation high, causing interest rates to levitate to compensate.

Some want to look at the low unemployment rate cost of getting inflation down (from a CPI peak of over 9%). That approach makes the Fed look more like it walks on water. But when you look at inflation relative to target and the capital markets, the Fed is no longer walking on water, it is drowning.

A warning from Ben Bernanke

At an AEA (American Economic Association) meeting early this year Ben Bernanke (former Fed Chair) was a panelist, and he remarked that since we have seen inflation after a long absence, people are going to be quicker to suspect inflation when we see price pressures arise. I think the current Fed chair should listen to the former Fed chair. Investors are buying gold and generally not acting as though the economy, and its fortunes, are playing out as the Fed suggests. Inflation expectations do not look anchored to me.

Wrapping up...

In addition to all that, there are all the different policies of the President that make the economic outlook even more perplexing, especially with the internecine policy battles Democrats and Republican are fighting. I cannot remember a time when I thought I knew what would happen next or what the risks are with a lower degree of certainty. I am having a hard time understanding the Fed's policies and priorities, as well as the policies and priorities of our two major political parties. Politics seems more like running a race with no finish line, where the object is to trip your opponent along the way rather than strive for your own best effort.

There is a load of domestic political chaos, more geopolitical destabilization, more dangerous actual hot wars in progress, as well as tariffs threatening, and a legacy of Covid excess spending catching up to us. The economic circumstances are, by themselves, hugely difficult to solve. The geopolitical layer cake of problems on top of these issues makes matters more intractable.

The calm...before the storm?

So why do markets seem so calm in the face of all this disruption and uncertainty? In the end it is trust that Trump will bring things together and faith in our political system, with its checks and balances, will keep the ball in the fairway. When it comes to financial markets, despite all the misplays by the Fed, and distortions of international capital flows, markets do not seem overly concerned about what the Fed does next. I think we chalk that up to the way the economy exited Covid and that, despite the monetary misplay on inflation, that the inflation spike did reverse quickly - if not fully. Many think that the grousing over inflation above 2% is overdone. Many are reassured by a Fed that is still looking for an opportunity to cut rates. That posture means the Fed has your back - at least for the short-run- if something 'bad' happens. Another thing this tells us is that while 'some' clearly have heightened inflation expectations, not all do. In two words: It's complicated.
